title: Intermittent by Design
summary: Roughly one in three AI requests fails for no apparent reason. Retrying the same request sometimes works, sometimes doesn't.

resolution.root_cause: A cross-region inference profile routes Bedrock InvokeModel calls across us-east-1, us-east-2, and us-west-2 for load balancing. A Service Control Policy applied by the parent organization blocks all API calls in us-east-2. When Bedrock routes to us-east-2, the call fails with AccessDeniedException. When it routes to an allowed region, it succeeds, producing an intermittent ~30% failure rate.

fix_criteria.description: Player identifies that the SCP blocks API calls in us-east-2
Player identifies that the cross-region inference profile includes us-east-2 as a destination region
Player connects the intermittent failure pattern to Bedrock's load-balancing across regions, some of which are SCP-blocked
Player proposes either updating the SCP to allow Bedrock in us-east-2 or switching to a geographic inference profile that only uses allowed regions

learning_objectives: How cross-region inference profiles in Amazon Bedrock route requests across multiple regions for load balancing
How Service Control Policies interact with cross-region AWS service features
Debugging intermittent failures by examining the routing layer rather than the application layer
Coordinating cloud governance policies (SCPs) with service-level configurations (inference profiles)
sop_steps: 1. Confirm the error rate. Check CloudWatch metrics to verify the 31.2% failure rate on Bedrock InvokeModel calls. InvokeModel is the API action that sends a prompt to an AI model and gets a response back.

2. Look at what the errors say. Pull the last 50 invocation log entries and confirm the failures all return AccessDeniedException with the message 'explicit deny in a service control policy.' This tells you the call was blocked by an organizational policy, not by the function's own permissions.
3. Notice which region the errors mention. The error messages reference the us-east-2 Bedrock endpoint ARN. The calls are being blocked specifically in that region.

4. Check where the inference profile sends requests. A cross-region inference profile spreads AI model requests across multiple AWS regions for higher throughput. Describe the profile (us.anthropic.claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022-v2:0) and list its destination regions: us-east-1, us-east-2, and us-west-2. You do not control which region handles any given request -- Bedrock's internal load balancer decides.

5. Check which regions the organization allows. Use organizations:ListPoliciesForTarget to list the Service Control Policies (SCPs) attached to the Polaris OU. An SCP is a policy set at the organization level that defines the maximum permissions for all accounts in a group. Identify RegionRestriction-v3.
6. Read the SCP. Confirm it denies all API actions (with exceptions for IAM, Organizations, STS, Support, and Budgets) in any region outside us-east-1 and us-west-2. The region us-east-2 is blocked.

7. Connect the dots. The inference profile routes to three regions. The SCP allows only two of those three. When Bedrock's load balancer picks us-east-2, the SCP blocks the call before it reaches Bedrock. When it picks us-east-1 or us-west-2, the call succeeds. One-third blocked equals roughly 31.2% failures.

8. Immediate fix: stop using cross-region routing. Switch the Lambda function to call the model directly in us-east-1 (a single allowed region). This eliminates the chance of routing to us-east-2 and restores 100% success rate, at the cost of losing cross-region load balancing.

9. Better fix: create a custom inference profile with only allowed regions. Use CreateInferenceProfile to build an application inference profile (one where you choose the regions) that includes only us-east-1 and us-west-2. Update the Lambda to use the new profile ARN. This restores cross-region load balancing without routing to blocked regions.
10. Verify the fix. Monitor CloudWatch InvocationErrors for 15 minutes and confirm the error rate drops to baseline (below 0.3%).

11. Prevent future conflicts. Work with the governance team to add a pre-deployment check that compares SCP region restrictions against active cross-region inference profiles. The SCP and the inference profile operate at different layers and neither validates against the other -- this gap is what caused the incident.
12. Document the incident. Record the timeline, root cause, and remediation in the post-incident review.

related_failure_modes.scenario: The SCP blocks Bedrock in every region, causing 100% failure instead of intermittent
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: If the SCP denied bedrock:InvokeModel in all regions -- including the ones the app normally uses -- every single request would fail, not just one-third. The error rate would be 100%, and the cause would be obvious. The intermittent pattern in this incident (31.2%) is what makes it deceptive: it looks like a service glitch rather than a policy conflict.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Before applying SCP region restrictions, make a list of all cross-region AWS features your teams use (inference profiles, S3 cross-region replication, DynamoDB global tables, etc.) and verify that every destination region stays on the approved list.

related_failure_modes.scenario: The IAM policy on the Lambda role is missing permission for one of the inference profile regions
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: If the IAM policy (the permission document attached to the Lambda function's role) only granted bedrock:InvokeModel for models in us-east-1 and us-west-2 but not us-east-2, the symptom would look similar -- intermittent AccessDeniedException at roughly one-third of requests. The clue is in the error message: it would say 'not authorized to perform' without mentioning 'service control policy.' The fix is updating the IAM policy, not the SCP or inference profile.
related_failure_modes.prevention: When setting up IAM policies for cross-region inference, include model ARNs for every region listed in the inference profile. This ensures the role has permission regardless of where Bedrock routes the request.

related_failure_modes.scenario: One of the inference profile regions is an opt-in region that was never enabled
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: Some AWS regions are not active by default -- you must explicitly enable them in your account settings (these are called opt-in regions). If a cross-region inference profile includes one of these and the account never enabled it, requests routed there fail. The error message may differ from an SCP block. The fix is to enable the region in account settings, not to change policies.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Before using a cross-region inference profile, check that every destination region is enabled in the account. Some regions (like af-south-1 or ap-east-1) require opt-in.

related_failure_modes.scenario: AWS Control Tower's region deny control blocks cross-region inference
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: Organizations using AWS Control Tower (a service for setting up and governing multi-account environments) may have a managed region deny control that works like an SCP but is managed by Control Tower, not created manually. The symptoms are identical, but you cannot edit the control directly. Instead, you must modify the Control Tower configuration or add a targeted SCP exception that overrides the managed control.
related_failure_modes.prevention: When enabling cross-region inference in a Control Tower environment, use the built-in mechanisms to extend region deny controls with Bedrock-specific exceptions. AWS documents this in their guide for enabling cross-region inference in multi-account setups.

sop_practices: Before applying SCP region restrictions, make a complete list of all cross-region AWS features your teams use -- inference profiles, S3 cross-region replication, DynamoDB global tables, Aurora global databases, and similar services that route traffic across regions. Verify that every destination region stays on the approved list. SCPs and cross-region features operate at different layers and neither validates against the other.

Use application inference profiles (custom profiles where you choose which regions to include) instead of system-defined profiles (fixed profiles with a preset region list) when your organization has region restrictions. This way you control exactly which regions Bedrock routes to, and you can align them with your organizational policies.

Add a pre-deployment validation step to the governance pipeline that automatically compares proposed SCP region changes against active cross-region service configurations in affected accounts. This catches conflicts before they cause intermittent failures in production.

Set up a CloudWatch alarm on Bedrock InvocationErrors as a percentage of InvocationCount. A stable, non-trivial error rate -- not near 0% and not near 100% -- is a strong signal of a routing or policy conflict rather than an application bug. In this case, the steady 31.2% rate was the key clue.

glossary.Cross-Region Inference Profile: A Bedrock configuration that routes model requests across multiple AWS regions for load balancing and higher throughput. System-defined profiles use a fixed set of regions. The caller does not choose which region handles a given request -- Bedrock's internal load balancer decides.
glossary.Service Control Policy (SCP): A policy set at the AWS Organizations level that defines the maximum permissions for all accounts in an organizational unit. SCPs cannot grant access -- they can only restrict it. If an SCP denies an action, no IAM policy in the child account can override it.
glossary.aws:RequestedRegion: A global IAM condition key that evaluates to the AWS region where an API call is being made. SCPs commonly use this key with StringNotEquals to deny actions in unapproved regions.
glossary.Organizational Unit (OU): A grouping of AWS accounts within AWS Organizations. Policies like SCPs can be attached to an OU, and every account inside that OU inherits those restrictions.
glossary.AccessDeniedException: An error returned when an AWS API call is blocked by IAM, SCP, or resource policies. The error message sometimes specifies 'explicit deny in a service control policy,' which means an SCP is the source of the block.
glossary.Application Inference Profile: A user-created Bedrock inference profile that allows custom region selection. Unlike system-defined profiles, you control exactly which regions are included, so you can exclude regions blocked by organizational policies.
glossary.InvokeModel: The Bedrock API action used to send a prompt to a foundation model and receive a response. When called with a cross-region inference profile, the request may be routed to any region listed in that profile.
glossary.Load Balancer Routing: The mechanism by which a service distributes requests across multiple destinations. In Bedrock cross-region inference, routing is heuristics-based -- requests are spread across destination regions for throughput and availability, with no caller control over which region is selected.

components.name: Amazon SQS (claims-intake-q)
components.role: Receives insurance claim documents from the intake system and triggers the Lambda processor
components.failure_impact: If the queue is inaccessible, claims stop entering the pipeline entirely

components.name: Lambda (claims-processor)
components.role: Reads claims from SQS, sends them to Bedrock for AI assessment via the cross-region inference profile, and returns results to adjusters
components.failure_impact: If the function errors, individual claims fail and retry up to three times before landing in the dead-letter queue

components.name: IAM Role (polaris-claims-exec)
components.role: Grants the Lambda function permission to call bedrock:InvokeModel on the inference profile and underlying foundation models in all three regions
components.failure_impact: If the IAM policy is too narrow, all Bedrock calls fail consistently -- not intermittently

components.name: Amazon Bedrock (cross-region inference profile)
components.role: Routes InvokeModel requests across us-east-1, us-east-2, and us-west-2 using internal load balancing for throughput and availability
components.failure_impact: If one destination region is blocked at the organizational policy level, requests routed there fail while requests to allowed regions succeed -- producing an intermittent failure pattern

components.name: SCP (RegionRestriction-v3)
components.role: Denies all API actions (except IAM, Organizations, STS, Support, Budgets) in any region outside us-east-1 and us-west-2, applied to the Polaris OU
connections: All API calls in Polaris OU accounts
components.failure_impact: Any Bedrock request routed to us-east-2 is denied before reaching the Bedrock service endpoint, returning AccessDeniedException

components.name: AWS Organizations (Polaris OU)
components.role: Groups the Polaris Underwriting production and dev accounts under Meridian Insurance Group, inheriting all attached SCPs
components.failure_impact: If an SCP is too broad, it can silently break cross-region service features without any deployment-time validation

system.data_flow: Claims arrive in the SQS queue. Lambda picks up each claim, assumes the polaris-claims-exec role, and calls bedrock:InvokeModel using the system-defined cross-region inference profile us.anthropic.claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022-v2:0. Bedrock's load balancer selects one of three destination regions (us-east-1, us-east-2, us-west-2) and routes the request there. Before the request reaches the Bedrock endpoint in the destination region, AWS Organizations evaluates the SCP attached to the Polaris OU. If the destination region is us-east-1 or us-west-2, the SCP allows the call and it succeeds. If the destination is us-east-2, the SCP denies the call and returns AccessDeniedException.
system.what_broke: On Tuesday, the Meridian governance team applied RegionRestriction-v3 to the Polaris OU, removing us-east-2 from the approved region list. The cross-region inference profile still includes us-east-2 as a destination. When Bedrock's load balancer routes a request to us-east-2, the SCP intercepts and denies it. Roughly one-third of all requests are routed there, producing a stable 31.2% failure rate. The SCP and the inference profile operate at different layers -- Organizations-level access control and Bedrock-level routing -- and neither validates against the other.

progressive_clues: failures are not random -- they follow a pattern, but the pattern is not in the request, it is in the routing
this account uses a cross-region inference profile -- where can Bedrock route the requests?
check which regions the inference profile includes, then check which regions the SCP allows
SCP blocks us-east-2, inference profile includes us-east-2 -- when Bedrock routes there the call is denied, when it routes to allowed regions it succeeds
